papers.title: Retrieving the Red Edge on Earth-like Planets with Heterogeneous Clouds and Surfaces
papers.abstract: The detection and characterization of potentially habitable exoplanets is one of the chief goals of astrophysics for the coming decades. Imaging in reflected light is well suited for characterizing Earth-like planets, as much can be learned about these planets in this wavelength range (i.e., 0.3-2 μm). Several studies have been conducted to determine the abilities and limitations of reflectance spectroscopy, but most previous studies assumed a homogeneous atmospheric and surface composition. Here we investigate how heterogeneities in the atmosphere and surface of an Earth-like planet impact retrieval results. We extend the ExoReL retrieval framework to include a step function for retrieving wavelength varying surface albedo. We then use it to retrieve on visible-to-near-infrared spectra of realistic 3D Earth models with different surface features in view and varying cloud types/distributions synthesized with the Planetary Spectrum Generator. Including the ability to fit for wavelength dependent albedo mitigates degeneracies that arise when using 1D models to analyze 3D planets, and we recover an Earth-like planet in all cases. We detect surface albedo steps at 0.7 and 1.1 μm despite clouds, both when significant lands are in view and when the spectra are averaged to account for a longer integration time. Our findings support the application of the vegetation red edge as a biosignature in the context of the Habitable Worlds Observatory. This study highlights the importance of considering a range of-particularly wavelength-dependent-surface albedos when using reflectance spectroscopy to characterize Earth-like exoplanets.
papers.summary:
**Why It Matters**

Imagine trying to figure out if a distant house is inhabited by looking at it through a telescope from miles away. That's essentially what astronomers face when searching for life on planets orbiting other stars. This research tackles one of humanity's most profound questions: Are we alone in the universe? Scientists are preparing for next-generation space telescopes that will be powerful enough to study the light reflected from Earth-like planets around other stars. By analyzing this light, they hope to detect signs of life—but first, they need to know what to look for and how reliably they can spot it.

**What They Did**

The researchers created virtual versions of Earth as it would appear from space, complete with realistic clouds, oceans, and continents in different arrangements. Think of it like creating a detailed movie of Earth from an alien's perspective. They then used computer models to simulate how starlight would bounce off these virtual Earths and what the reflected light would tell us. Crucially, they made their models messy and realistic—unlike previous studies that assumed planets would look uniform, they included patchy clouds and mixed surface types, just like our own planet. They developed new techniques to analyze the light signatures, specifically looking for the "red edge"—a telltale change in how vegetation reflects light that could indicate plant life.

**Key Findings**

Even with realistic, messy conditions including clouds and mixed surfaces, the team successfully identified Earth-like characteristics in every test case. Most importantly, they detected the vegetation red edge—the light signature of plant life—at wavelengths of 0.7 and 1.1 micrometers, even when clouds partially obscured the surface. This signal appeared both when continents dominated the view and when they averaged the light over longer observation periods, mimicking how real telescope observations would work.

**Looking Forward**

These results are encouraging news for the upcoming Habitable Worlds Observatory, a proposed space telescope designed to search for life on distant planets. The research suggests that this vegetation signature could serve as a reliable "biosignature"—a sign of life that we can detect from light-years away, opening new possibilities for discovering life beyond Earth.

tweet_hook.hook: 🌍 We might be looking for alien life all wrong
tweet_hook.claim: New research reveals that Earth-like planets have patchy clouds and mixed surfaces that could hide signs of life from our telescopes - including the 'red edge' signature of vegetation
tweet_hook.evidence: Previous studies assumed uniform planet surfaces, but real worlds are messy - and that changes everything for detecting life
tweet_hook.question: What if we've been missing habitable worlds because we expected them to look too 'perfect'?

papers.title: Three outstanding physical questions for K2-18 b and other temperate sub-Neptunes
papers.abstract: Recent transmission spectra of the temperate sub-Neptune K2-18 b obtained with JWST have attracted significant attention. Debates have quickly arisen over the interpretation of the spectral data, particularly the recent MIRI observation where dimethyl sulfide (DMS) and dimethyl disulfide (DMDS) are claimed. Here we revisit K2-18 b as a case study to examine several key questions that are also broadly relevant to the temperate sub-Neptune population: i) Can the low water abundance be reconciled with water clouds driven by orbital eccentricity? ii) Are the observed and non-observed atmospheric compositions mutually consistent? iii) Is it kinetically possible to produce DMS under sub-Neptune conditions? To address these questions, we couple climate and photochemical models to obtain self-consistent climate-photochemistry states for K2-18 b with a moderate orbital eccentricity of 0.2, as suggested by radial-velocity measurements. In addition, we present new laboratory measurements of DMS and DMDS infrared opacities by HFML-FELIX and compile updated C₂H₆ (ethane) opacities that include weak overtone bands. Our results support the interpretation of a sub-Neptune scenario without invoking DMS, and we do not find strong evidence for a water-rich interior.
papers.summary:
**Why It Matters**

Imagine finding a world that sits right in the "Goldilocks zone" around its star—not too hot, not too cold, but just right for liquid water to exist. That's exactly what astronomers thought they had with K2-18 b, a planet about 120 light-years away that's roughly twice the size of Earth. Recent observations using the James Webb Space Telescope sparked intense debate when scientists claimed to detect dimethyl sulfide—a gas that, on Earth, is only produced by living organisms. The discovery would be groundbreaking, potentially representing our first detection of biological activity beyond our solar system. But extraordinary claims require extraordinary evidence, and this new research takes a closer look at whether these exciting conclusions hold up under scrutiny.

**What They Did**

The research team acted like cosmic detectives, using computer models to simulate K2-18 b's atmosphere and climate under different conditions. They combined weather models (similar to those used for Earth's climate predictions) with chemistry models that track how different gases interact in the planet's atmosphere. The scientists also conducted new laboratory experiments, measuring how dimethyl sulfide and other molecules absorb infrared light—the same type of light the space telescope detected. Think of it like creating a reference library of molecular "fingerprints" to better identify what gases are actually present in the planet's atmosphere.

**Key Findings**

The team found three crucial problems with the biological interpretation. First, the planet's slightly oval orbit should create enough temperature variation to form water clouds, but the telescope data shows surprisingly little water vapor. Second, the atmospheric chemistry doesn't add up—the gases that were detected and those that weren't don't form a consistent chemical picture. Most importantly, their models suggest it's extremely difficult, if not impossible, for dimethyl sulfide to form naturally in this planet's atmospheric conditions, even without biological processes.

**Looking Forward**

This research demonstrates both the promise and challenges of searching for life beyond Earth. While K2-18 b may not harbor the biological signatures initially claimed, the study advances our understanding of sub-Neptune planets—a common type of world with no equivalent in our solar system. As telescope technology improves, scientists are developing more sophisticated tools to distinguish between genuine biosignatures and misleading atmospheric phenomena, bringing us closer to answering one of humanity's most profound questions: are we alone?

tweet_hook.hook: 🚨 JWST just detected something wild on exoplanet K2-18 b that has astronomers feuding
tweet_hook.claim: Scientists are debating whether JWST found potential signs of life (dimethyl sulfide) in the atmosphere of a distant water world 124 light-years away
tweet_hook.evidence: The same molecules that give rotting seaweed its smell could be biosignatures - but the water levels don't add up like we'd expect
tweet_hook.question: Are we looking at alien biology or just really confusing atmospheric chemistry? 🤔

papers.title: A Planetary Illusion's Funeral: Non-detection of a Gaia DR3 Exoplanet Candidate, and the Role of Intermediate-precision Radial Velocities in Gaia Exoplanet Follow-up
papers.abstract: The detection of exoplanets using astrometry has long been an area of interest, but is fraught with challenges. The Gaia mission is fundamentally reshaping this field thanks to its unprecedentedly precise all-sky astrometric observations. The 2022 release of Gaia DR3 brought the first exoplanets discovered from the Gaia astrometry, including a new candidate around the bright (V=6.6) solar-type star HD 12800. However, two years after announcement, the Gaia exoplanet candidate was retracted. In this work we report radial velocity observations of HD 12800 acquired with the TRES spectrograph, which we began immediately after the release of Gaia DR3. Our observations failed to detect the planet candidate; nonetheless, we emphasise that the originally proposed companion would have been easily detected in our radial velocity observations. We conclude with a discussion on the role of intermediate-precision (≈10 m s⁻¹) RV spectrographs in the follow-up of Gaia astrometric exoplanet candidates, relevant to the forthcoming release of Gaia DR4. We argue that such observations may play an important role in planet confirmation for stars between approximately 8<G<12, likely to represent a significant fraction of Gaia exoplanet discoveries.
papers.summary:
**Why It Matters**

Finding planets around other stars is one of the most exciting frontiers in astronomy, helping us understand how common Earth-like worlds might be in our galaxy. The European Space Agency's Gaia spacecraft has been revolutionizing this hunt by measuring the tiny wobbles that planets cause in their host stars' positions—like detecting how a person might sway slightly while walking a large, energetic dog on a leash. When Gaia announced its first exoplanet discoveries in 2022, including a potential planet around a Sun-like star called HD 12800, it marked a major milestone. However, science is self-correcting, and sometimes what initially looks like a groundbreaking discovery turns out to be an illusion—which is exactly what happened here.

**What They Did**

The researchers used a ground-based telescope spectrograph called TRES to take a completely different kind of measurement of HD 12800. While Gaia watches for tiny changes in a star's position in the sky, TRES measures changes in the star's motion toward or away from Earth by analyzing how the star's light shifts color—similar to how an ambulance siren changes pitch as it moves toward or away from you. If a planet was really orbiting HD 12800, it would cause the star to move in a predictable pattern that both techniques should detect. The team collected observations over time to see if they could confirm the planet's existence using this independent method.

**Key Findings**

The ground-based observations delivered disappointing but scientifically important news: there was no sign of the proposed planet around HD 12800. The researchers were confident they would have easily spotted the planet if it existed as originally described. This confirms that Gaia's initial detection was likely a false alarm—what astronomers call a "planetary illusion." The original candidate has since been officially retracted from the scientific literature.

**Looking Forward**

This work isn't just about one failed planet detection; it's about improving how we confirm future discoveries. The researchers demonstrate that medium-precision ground-based telescopes will be crucial for verifying the flood of potential planets that Gaia will announce in its next data release. This combination of space-based detection and ground-based confirmation represents the future of reliable exoplanet discovery.

tweet_hook.hook: 🪦 RIP to another 'exoplanet' that never existed
tweet_hook.claim: Scientists thought they'd found a new planet around a bright star using Europe's Gaia telescope, but had to retract it 2 years later when follow-up observations showed it was a false alarm
tweet_hook.evidence: The 'planet' around HD 12800 (a star you can see with binoculars) fooled initial detection algorithms but couldn't survive rigorous testing
tweet_hook.question: How many other 'planets' in our databases might just be cosmic mirages? 🤔

papers.title: A Century of Radial Velocity and Astrometric Monitoring of 70 Oph AB: New PFS Data and Constraints on Planetary Companions
papers.abstract: At a distance of 5.1 pc, the 70 Oph AB binary star system is one of the most favorable targets for future direct imaging and astrometry missions surveying mature, terrestrial planets. We present new radial velocities (RVs) obtained with the Planet Finder Spectrograph (PFS) on the 6.5\,m Magellan II Clay Telescope in Chile. We collected 499 measurements of 70 Oph A and 334 measurements of 70 Oph B during 2023--2025. Combining these data with decades of archival RVs and astrometry, we derive an updated orbital solution for the binary and dynamical masses of 0.88 ± 0.004\,M_☉ and 0.73 ± 0.003\,M_☉ for the primary and secondary components, respectively. We find that the long-term RV variability of both components is consistent with stellar activity modulated by rotation periods, and we detect no coherent planetary signals in either component. We place upper limits on any planets orbiting in the plane of the binary. The 27 yr RV baseline for 70 Oph A excludes Jupiter-mass planets interior to 5 au and reaches a sensitivity of 0.3\,M Juₚ at 1 au or 0.5\,M Juₚ at 2 au. For 70 Oph B, with PFS data we rule out planets more massive than 0.25--0.3\,M Juₚ inside 0.5 au. We show that stable S-type orbits around 70 Oph A extend to 2.5 au, covering the habitable zone. Thus, Saturn-mass planets or smaller on stable orbits in the habitable zone of 70 Oph A are allowed. Overall, our results provide important guidance for future planet searches around this stellar system.
papers.summary:
**Why It Matters**

Imagine having cosmic neighbors just down the street—that's essentially what the 70 Ophiuchi star system represents to astronomers. Located a mere 17 light-years away (practically next door in space terms), this pair of stars has captivated scientists for over a century. Why the fascination? This system sits in the perfect sweet spot for future space missions designed to directly photograph Earth-like planets orbiting other stars—something we've never achieved before. Understanding what kinds of planets might exist there helps scientists plan these ambitious missions and brings us closer to answering one of humanity's biggest questions: Are there other worlds like Earth?

**What They Did**

For nearly three decades, astronomers have been playing cosmic detective with this star system, using a technique called radial velocity monitoring. Picture how an ambulance siren changes pitch as it moves toward or away from you—stars do something similar with light when planets orbit them. The gravitational tug of orbiting planets causes stars to wobble ever so slightly, shifting their light toward red or blue. Using the powerful Planet Finder Spectrograph in Chile, researchers collected hundreds of precise measurements of both stars between 2023-2025, combining this fresh data with decades of previous observations to create the most complete picture yet of this stellar duo.

**Key Findings**

The good news and bad news came hand in hand. The bad news: despite a century of searching, no planets have been definitively detected around either star. However, the researchers didn't come away empty-handed. They've now ruled out the presence of Jupiter-sized planets close to both stars and determined that any planets larger than about one-third of Jupiter's mass simply don't exist in the inner regions. Most intriguingly, they confirmed that Saturn-sized planets or smaller could still be hiding in the "habitable zone" around the brighter star—the region where liquid water could exist on a planet's surface.

**Looking Forward**

This research provides a crucial roadmap for upcoming space telescopes designed to directly image exoplanets. Knowing that smaller, potentially habitable worlds might exist around 70 Ophiuchi A makes this system a prime target for these next-generation missions, bringing us one step closer to finding another Earth.

tweet_hook.hook: 🔭 Scientists just spent a CENTURY watching two nearby stars—and what they found is incredible!
tweet_hook.claim: After 100 years of monitoring the 70 Ophiuchi star system (just 17 light-years away), astronomers have gathered unprecedented data that could reveal hidden planets ripe for future exploration.
tweet_hook.evidence: Using Chile's massive 6.5-meter telescope, they collected 833 precise measurements over 2 years, adding to decades of observations.
tweet_hook.question: Could this nearby star system be hiding Earth-like worlds we'll soon be able to photograph directly?

papers.title: Observations of Exocomets
papers.abstract: Active small bodies in extrasolar systems, the extrasolar analogues of Solar System comets, provide insights into the orbital evolution and physical processes shaping planetary systems. Since the discovery of exocomets around β Pictoris, these small, icy bodies have shown the potential to become key probes for understanding planetary formation and migration. This review presents an overview of current observational techniques used to detect exocomets, focusing on individual systems and large-scale searches. We discuss photometric methods that identify exocomet transits through asymmetric light curves and spectroscopic techniques revealing cometary gases via time-variable absorption lines. Despite progress, significant open questions remain regarding the physical properties, occurrence rates, and similarities between exocomets and their Solar System counterparts. This review explores future opportunities in observational exocomet research, highlighting advancements required to further our understanding of these active small bodies and their role in the context of planetary system evolution.
papers.summary:
**Why It Matters**

Imagine trying to understand how neighborhoods form and change over time by studying not just the houses, but also the delivery trucks, stray cats, and scattered newspapers that move between them. That's essentially what astronomers are doing when they study exocomets—icy, rocky chunks that zip around distant star systems like cosmic messengers. These "dirty snowballs," as comets are often called, carry crucial clues about how planets form and migrate over millions of years. Just as studying the small details of a neighborhood reveals hidden patterns of life, observing these tiny wanderers helps us understand the grand story of how solar systems evolve. Since we've only got one solar system to study up close—our own—finding and analyzing exocomets around other stars gives us a much broader perspective on whether our cosmic neighborhood is typical or wonderfully weird.

**What They Did**

This research reviews the detective work astronomers use to spot exocomets, which is like trying to identify individual raindrops during a storm while standing miles away. Scientists employ two main techniques: watching for tiny dips in starlight when comets pass in front of their stars (like noticing a moth briefly blocking a distant streetlight), and analyzing the "fingerprints" of gases that comets release as they heat up near their stars. These fingerprints appear as dark lines in the star's light spectrum, created when comet gases absorb specific colors of starlight. The challenge is enormous—these icy bodies are incredibly small and faint compared to the blazing stars they orbit.

**Key Findings**

The first exocomet was discovered around Beta Pictoris, a young star about 63 light-years away, opening an entirely new field of study. Since then, astronomers have detected exocomets in several star systems, though the exact count remains uncertain due to the difficulty of confirming these observations. The research reveals that current methods can detect large swarms of comets, particularly around young stars where comet activity tends to be more dramatic. However, scientists still can't definitively answer fundamental questions: How common are exocomets? Are they similar to the comets in our solar system? The data suggests these cosmic wanderers are more common than previously thought, especially in younger star systems.

**Looking Forward**

This research sets the stage for a revolution in understanding planetary systems. As telescope technology advances, astronomers expect to detect exocomets around many more stars, potentially revealing universal patterns in how solar systems develop. These discoveries could help answer whether our solar system's structure—with its neat arrangement of rocky inner planets and gas giant outer planets—is common or rare in the universe, ultimately informing our understanding of how likely Earth-like worlds might be.

tweet_hook.hook: 🌟 Scientists are tracking 'dirty snowballs' orbiting alien stars millions of miles away!
tweet_hook.claim: Exocomets - icy bodies like our Solar System's comets - are giving us unprecedented glimpses into how planets form and move around distant stars
tweet_hook.evidence: Since discovering the first exocomets around β Pictoris, these cosmic time capsules reveal planetary migration patterns
tweet_hook.question: What secrets about alien worlds are hidden in these frozen relics? 🧊

papers.title: The Arc in the DX Cha Circumbinary System: Evidence For a Retrograde Circumbinary Disk
papers.abstract: Observations of the binary system DX Cha (HD 104237) reveal a compact, asymmetric ring structure with a radius of 0.43\,au. This ring is just outside the binary orbit, which has semi-major axis a b = 0.22\,au and eccentricity e b = 0.665; placing the ring at ≈ 1.2 times the binary apocenter distance. The inner regions of circumbinary disks, ≈ 2-3\,a b, are typically evacuated by strong gravitational torques from the binary, resulting in a deep gap between the binary and the disk. Accordingly, previous numerical simulations of DX Cha have found an eccentric inner cavity with almost no material inside ≈ 1\,au, and we find similar results when making the same assumption that the circumbinary disk orbits in the same direction as the binary. However, the disk can exist much closer to the binary if it is retrograde. For DX Cha we find that the inner edge of a retrograde disk occurs at ≈ 2a b, and moreover takes the form of one or two arcs, in agreement with observations. We therefore suggest that the circumbinary disk in the DX Cha system could be orbiting retrograde to the binary star system in the center. We conclude that compact circumbinary disks observed in young stellar systems are important targets for future observations; if the disks are prograde then their properties are likely to be significantly different from current estimates, while if they are retrograde then this will have profound implications for our understanding of star and planet formation.
papers.summary:
**Why It Matters**

Imagine a cosmic dance where planets and stars spin in opposite directions—like figure skaters rotating against each other rather than in harmony. This unusual scenario might be happening in a stellar system called DX Cha, located about 600 light-years from Earth. Understanding how planets form around pairs of stars (called binary systems) is crucial because roughly half of all star systems in our galaxy contain multiple stars. If this system is indeed spinning "backward," it could revolutionize our understanding of how planets are born and challenge our assumptions about the cosmic choreography that creates new worlds.

**What They Did**

Scientists studied DX Cha, a system containing two stars orbiting each other, surrounded by a disk of gas and dust—the raw material from which planets eventually form. Using powerful telescopes, they measured the disk's structure and compared their observations to computer simulations. They ran two different scenarios: one where the disk spins in the same direction as the binary stars (called "prograde"), and another where it spins in the opposite direction ("retrograde"). Think of it like testing whether a merry-go-round works better when spinning the same way as nearby playground equipment, or in the opposite direction.

**Key Findings**

The disk sits remarkably close to the binary stars—just 1.2 times farther than the stars' most distant point from each other. This proximity was puzzling because gravitational forces from spinning binary stars typically clear out nearby space, like a cosmic vacuum cleaner. However, when researchers modeled a backward-spinning disk, everything clicked into place. The retrograde scenario perfectly explained both the disk's compact size and its distinctive arc-like shape, which wouldn't exist if the disk spun in the same direction as the stars.

**Looking Forward**

This discovery suggests we may need to rethink planet formation around binary stars. If confirmed, retrograde disks could form planets with completely different characteristics than previously thought. Future telescope observations of similar compact disks around binary systems could reveal whether DX Cha is unique or part of a previously unknown class of planetary nurseries spinning against the cosmic tide.
papers.summary_html:
Why It Matters

Imagine a cosmic dance where planets and stars spin in opposite directions—like figure skaters rotating against each other rather than in harmony. This unusual scenario might be happening in a stellar system called DX Cha, located about 600 light-years from Earth. Understanding how planets form around pairs of stars (called binary systems) is crucial because roughly half of all star systems in our galaxy contain multiple stars. If this system is indeed spinning "backward," it could revolutionize our understanding of how planets are born and challenge our assumptions about the cosmic choreography that creates new worlds.

What They Did

Scientists studied DX Cha, a system containing two stars orbiting each other, surrounded by a disk of gas and dust—the raw material from which planets eventually form. Using powerful telescopes, they measured the disk's structure and compared their observations to computer simulations. They ran two different scenarios: one where the disk spins in the same direction as the binary stars (called "prograde"), and another where it spins in the opposite direction ("retrograde"). Think of it like testing whether a merry-go-round works better when spinning the same way as nearby playground equipment, or in the opposite direction.

Key Findings

The disk sits remarkably close to the binary stars—just 1.2 times farther than the stars' most distant point from each other. This proximity was puzzling because gravitational forces from spinning binary stars typically clear out nearby space, like a cosmic vacuum cleaner. However, when researchers modeled a backward-spinning disk, everything clicked into place. The retrograde scenario perfectly explained both the disk's compact size and its distinctive arc-like shape, which wouldn't exist if the disk spun in the same direction as the stars.

Looking Forward

This discovery suggests we may need to rethink planet formation around binary stars. If confirmed, retrograde disks could form planets with completely different characteristics than previously thought. Future telescope observations of similar compact disks around binary systems could reveal whether DX Cha is unique or part of a previously unknown class of planetary nurseries spinning against the cosmic tide.

tweet_hook.hook: 🌌 Scientists found a disk of material orbiting backwards around a double star system!
tweet_hook.claim: The DX Cha binary system has a ring of dust and gas spinning in the opposite direction to the two stars it surrounds - defying our expectations of how these systems should work.
tweet_hook.evidence: The ring sits incredibly close at just 1.2 times the stars' maximum separation distance, where gravitational forces should have cleared it out.
tweet_hook.question: How did this cosmic rebel manage to survive so close to two gravitational bullies?
